metadata.description: Vocabulary from Integrated Chinese Lesson 14 Dialogue II: durations of time, describing facial features, zodiac signs, and complimenting someone.

content.name: hour
content.side_e: Sān ge bàn zhōngtóu.
content.side_f: Three and a half hours.
content.side_g: 钟头 is the colloquial equivalent of 小时 (xiǎoshí, hour). Pattern: number + 个 + 钟头, e.g. 三个钟头 = 'three hours.' Note the obligatory 个 — this is one of the few duration words that needs the measure. 小时 is more formal/written, 钟头 more casual/spoken. Don't confuse with 钟 (clock) or 一点钟 (one o'clock).

content.name: to_assume_wrongly
content.side_a: To assume erroneously; to mistakenly think
content.side_e: Wǒ yǐwéi tā yǐjīng lái le.
content.side_f: I (mistakenly) thought she had already arrived.
content.side_g: 以为 carries the implication that the speaker was wrong — 'I thought X, but turns out not.' Critical contrast with 觉得 (juéde, feel/think) and 认为 (rènwéi, believe), which are neutral. Saying 我以为你是日本人 means 'I had assumed you were Japanese (and was wrong)' — sometimes a polite correction-prompt.

content.name: smart
content.side_e: Lǎoshī shuō nǐ yòu cōngming yòu yònggōng.
content.side_f: The teacher says you're both smart and hard-working.
content.side_g: 聪 means 'sharp of hearing' and 明 means 'sharp of sight' — together, perceptive and quick-witted. Common compliment for kids and students. Pair with 又…又… (both...and...) as in 又聪明又用功. Stronger forms: 很聪明, 真聪明, 非常聪明. Opposite: 笨 (bèn, dumb) — avoid using to anyone's face.

content.name: hardworking
content.side_e: Tā xuéxí hěn yònggōng.
content.side_f: He studies very diligently.
content.side_g: 用 = 'apply/use' + 功 = 'effort,' so 用功 is 'apply effort,' specifically toward studies. Almost always about academics or skill practice — not generic work effort (use 努力 nǔlì for that). Highest praise for a student. Often paired with 聪明 in 又聪明又用功.

content.name: summer_term
content.side_e: Wǒ zài shǔqī bān xué de Zhōngwén.
content.side_f: I learned Chinese in summer school.
content.side_g: 暑 means 'hot/summer heat' and 期 means 'period.' 暑期 refers to the summer academic session. Compound 暑期班 = summer class/program. Compare with 寒假 (hánjià, winter break) and 暑假 (shǔjià, summer break) — 期 implies a structured term, 假 implies vacation.

content.name: class_group
content.side_e: Wǒ de Zhōngwén bān yǒu shíwǔ ge xuésheng.
content.side_f: My Chinese class has fifteen students.
content.side_g: 班 is the group of students who take a course together — distinct from 课 (kè), which is the course itself or a session. 我有电脑课 = 'I have a computer class today' (the session); 我的电脑班有二十人 = 'my computer class has twenty people' (the cohort). Also: 上班 (go to work), 下班 (get off work), 早班 (morning shift).

content.name: to_grow
content.side_a: To grow; to appear; to develop
content.side_e: Tāngmǔ zhǎng de zhēn kě'ài.
content.side_f: Tom looks really cute (lit. 'has grown really cute').
content.side_g: Same character has two readings: zhǎng (verb 'to grow, to appear') and cháng (adjective 'long'). 长得 + adjective = 'looks/appears [adj],' as in 长得很漂亮 'looks very pretty.' Also in 长大 (grow up). The reading switches by context — 长大 is zhǎng dà, but 长长的 (very long) is cháng cháng de.

content.name: cute
content.side_e: Tāngmǔ zhǎng de zhēn kě'ài!
content.side_f: Tom is really cute!
content.side_g: 可 = 'able to be' + 爱 = 'love,' so 可爱 = 'lovable/cute.' Used for kids, animals, and things — not typically for adults you're attracted to (use 漂亮 piàoliang or 帅 shuài instead). Common compliment to a baby or pet. Often emphasized: 太可爱了!, 真可爱.

content.name: last_year
content.side_e: Tā shì qùnián shēng de.
content.side_f: She/he was born last year.
content.side_g: 去 = 'past/gone' + 年 = 'year.' Set: 去年 (last year), 今年 (this year), 明年 (next year), 前年 (year before last), 后年 (year after next). Time words like 去年 don't take 在 — say 去年我去了北京, not 在去年. Position: typically before the verb or at sentence start.

content.name: to_belong_zodiac
content.side_a: To belong to (a Chinese zodiac sign); to be born in the year of
content.side_e: Wǒ shǔ gǒu, nǐ shǔ shénme?
content.side_f: I'm a Dog (zodiac); what's yours?
content.side_g: 属 in this birthday-party context means 'to be born in the year of [zodiac animal].' Pattern: 属 + animal — 属狗 (Dog), 属龙 (Dragon), 属虎 (Tiger). Twelve animals total: 鼠 牛 虎 兔 龙 蛇 马 羊 猴 鸡 狗 猪. Common small-talk question: 你属什么? 属 also has the everyday meaning 'belong to' (this 属于 yúshǔ).

content.name: dog
content.side_e: Tā shǔ gǒu, jīnnián shíwǔ suì.
content.side_f: He's a Dog (zodiac); he's fifteen this year.
content.side_g: 狗 = dog. Measure word 一只狗 (one dog) — 只 (zhī) for most animals. The Dog is the 11th zodiac sign (people born in 1958, 1970, 1982, 1994, 2006, 2018, 2030...). In idioms 狗 usually carries negative connotations (狗屁 = nonsense; 走狗 = lackey), but 小狗 (puppy) is purely affectionate.

content.name: face
content.side_e: Tā de liǎn yuán yuán de.
content.side_f: His face is round.
content.side_g: 脸 is the physical face. Reduplicated adjectives like 圆圆的, 大大的 attach with 的 to describe features in a soft, descriptive way. Important compound: 丢脸 (diūliǎn, lose face/be embarrassed) and 没脸 (méi liǎn, no face/shameless) — Chinese 'face' culture (面子) is built on this character. Don't confuse with 面 (miàn, face/surface, more formal).

content.name: round
content.side_e: Tā de liǎn yuán yuán de, hěn kě'ài.
content.side_f: His face is nice and round, very cute.
content.side_g: 圆 means literally round (geometric circle) and figuratively 'complete/satisfying.' Reduplicated 圆圆的 softens the descriptor — common for friendly facial-feature descriptions. Also a noun meaning 'circle' (圆形 yuánxíng = circular shape) and the unit of currency 元 / 圆 (yuán). Sounds the same as 元 — same currency, often interchanged.

content.name: eye
content.side_e: Tā de yǎnjing dà dà de.
content.side_f: His eyes are big.
content.side_g: 眼睛 is the everyday word for 'eyes,' even when referring to one. Distinct from 眼镜 (yǎnjìng, eyeglasses) — same pinyin different second character and tone. Measure word 一双眼睛 (a pair) or 一只眼睛 (one eye). 大眼睛 (big eyes) is a stock compliment in Chinese.

content.name: nose
content.side_e: Tā de bízi gāo gāo de.
content.side_f: His nose is high (prominent).
content.side_g: 鼻 is the bare character; 鼻子 (with the noun-ifying 子 suffix) is the everyday word. 鼻子高 ('high nose') is a positive feature in Chinese aesthetics — refers to a prominent nose bridge. Compounds: 鼻血 (nosebleed), 鼻涕 (snot), 鼻塞 (stuffy nose).

content.name: mouth
content.side_e: Tā de zuǐ bú dà yě bù xiǎo.
content.side_f: His mouth is neither big nor small.
content.side_g: 嘴 is the physical mouth, distinct from 口 (kǒu) which is more abstract (口语 spoken language, 一口井 a well). 嘴巴 (zuǐba) is a more colloquial form. Idioms: 闭嘴 (shut up), 嘴硬 (stubborn-mouthed), 多嘴 (talk too much). Reduplication 嘴小小的 = 'small little mouth.'

content.name: to_resemble
content.side_a: To be like; to look like; to take after
content.side_e: Tā zhǎng de hěn xiàng Hǎilún.
content.side_f: He looks a lot like Helen.
content.side_g: 像 = 'resemble' for physical or behavioral likeness. Pattern: 长得像 + person ('look like'), 像…一样 ('just like...'). Also a noun: 照像 (take a photo), 像片 (photograph). Don't confuse with 象 (xiàng, elephant; symbol) — different character, sometimes interchangeable in older texts.

content.name: to_grow_up
content.side_e: Érzi zhǎng dà yídìng yě hěn shuài.
content.side_f: When the son grows up he'll definitely be handsome too.
content.side_g: 长 (grow) + 大 (big) = 'grow up.' Verb-complement structure — 大 modifies the result of 长. Often used about children: 长大以后 = 'after growing up,' 长大成人 = 'grown into an adult.' Past tense: 长大了 (have grown up). 我在北京长大 = 'I grew up in Beijing.'

content.name: certainly
content.side_e: Tā zhǎng dà yídìng hěn shuài.
content.side_f: When he grows up he'll definitely be handsome.
content.side_g: 一定 modifies a verb or adjective to express certainty — 'definitely.' Position: before the predicate. Negative form 不一定 = 'not necessarily.' Also as an adjective meaning 'specific/fixed,' as in 一定的时间 'a certain amount of time.' Stronger than 可能 (kěnéng, possibly) and 应该 (yīnggāi, should).

content.name: cake
content.side_e: Wǒmen chī dàngāo ba!
content.side_f: Let's eat cake!
content.side_g: 蛋 = 'egg' + 糕 = 'cake/pastry,' so literally 'egg cake.' Specific kinds: 生日蛋糕 (birthday cake), 巧克力蛋糕 (chocolate), 奶油蛋糕 (cream). Measure word: 一块蛋糕 (a slice), 一个蛋糕 (a whole cake). 切蛋糕 = cut the cake (the ritual moment at birthday parties).

content.name: most
content.side_e: Tā zuì ài chī dàngāo.
content.side_f: She loves cake the most.
content.side_g: 最 is the superlative marker, placed before adjectives or stative verbs: 最好 (best), 最大 (biggest), 最爱 (love most). Equivalent to English '-est' or 'most.' Compare with 更 (gèng, more — comparative) and 比较 (bǐjiào, relatively). 最 + adjective + 的 + noun = 'the [adj]est [noun].'

content.name: helen
content.side_e: Zhè shì wǒ biǎojiě Hǎilún.
content.side_f: This is my older cousin Helen.
content.side_g: 海伦 is the standard Chinese transliteration of 'Helen' — 海 (hǎi, sea) + 伦 (lún, ethics/order). Foreign names in Chinese are typically transliterated with characters chosen for pleasant meaning, not just sound. In the textbook narrative, 海伦 is Wang Peng's older female cousin (表姐) and Tom's mother.

content.name: tom
content.side_e: Tāngmǔ gāng yí suì, shì qùnián shēng de.
content.side_f: Tom is just one year old; he was born last year.
content.side_g: 汤姆 is the standard Chinese transliteration of 'Tom' — 汤 (tāng, soup) + 姆 (mǔ, used in foreign-name transliterations). In the textbook, 汤姆 is Helen's young son (Wang Peng's biǎojiě's son). The character 姆 appears in many female-coded transliterations like 玛姆 / 阿姆.

content.name: christmas
content.side_e: Shèngdàn jié wǒmen yìbān gēn jiārén yìqǐ guò.
content.side_f: We usually spend Christmas together with family.
content.side_g: 圣 (shèng, holy/saint) + 诞 (dàn, birth) + 节 (jié, festival), literally 'Holy-Birth Festival.' Pair with 过 to talk about celebrating: 过圣诞节. Related: 圣诞老人 (Shèngdàn lǎorén, Santa Claus), 圣诞树 (Shèngdàn shù, Christmas tree), 圣诞快乐 (Shèngdàn kuàilè, Merry Christmas). Christmas Eve is 平安夜 (Píng'ān yè, lit. 'peaceful night').

content.name: leg
content.side_e: Wǒ de tuǐ hěn cháng.
content.side_f: My legs are very long.
content.side_g: 腿 covers the whole leg (thigh + lower leg). 大腿 (dàtuǐ) = thigh, 小腿 (xiǎotuǐ) = calf/shin. Measure word: 条 (tiáo) — 两条腿 (two legs). Useful: 跑腿 (pǎotuǐ, run errands), 腿疼 (tuǐ téng, leg hurts), 翘腿 (qiào tuǐ, cross one's legs).

content.name: foot
content.side_e: Wǒ de jiǎo hěn lěng.
content.side_f: My feet are very cold.
content.side_g: 脚 is the foot only (ankle down) — distinct from 腿 (the whole leg). Measure word: 只 (zhī) — 两只脚 (two feet). Compounds: 脚踝 (jiǎohuái, ankle), 脚跟 (jiǎogēn, heel), 脚步 (jiǎobù, footstep). Idiom: 三脚猫 (sānjiǎomāo, lit. 'three-legged cat,' = jack of all trades).

content.name: toe
content.side_e: Wǒ de jiǎozhǐ tòng.
content.side_f: My toes hurt.
content.side_g: 脚 (jiǎo, foot) + 趾 (zhǐ, digit). The 趾 character marks toes; for fingers, use 手指 (shǒuzhǐ, hand + digit). Big toe = 大脚趾 (dà jiǎozhǐ); little toe = 小脚趾 (xiǎo jiǎozhǐ). Toenail = 脚趾甲 (jiǎozhǐjiǎ).

content.name: hand
content.side_e: Qǐng xǐ shǒu zài chīfàn.
content.side_f: Please wash your hands before eating.
content.side_g: 手 is a pictograph of a hand. Measure word: 只 (zhī) — 两只手 (two hands). Productive compounds: 手机 (shǒujī, cell phone, 'hand-machine'), 手指 (shǒuzhǐ, finger), 握手 (wòshǒu, shake hands), 拿手 (náshǒu, expert at), 帮手 (bāngshǒu, helper).

content.name: ear
content.side_e: Tā de ěrduo hěn dà.
content.side_f: His ears are very big.
content.side_g: 耳 (ěr, ear) + 朵 (duo, a soft noun suffix; toneless in this word). The pattern of 耳朵 / 鼻子 / 嘴巴 — adding a suffix to a single-character body part — makes the noun feel more natural in spoken Chinese. In compounds, 耳 alone is used: 耳机 (ěrjī, headphones), 耳鸣 (ěrmíng, tinnitus), 耳环 (ěrhuán, earring).

content.name: head
content.side_e: Wǒ tóu tòng.
content.side_f: I have a headache. (lit. My head hurts.)
content.side_g: 头 means 'head' as a body part, but it's also a productive noun suffix meaning 'end / side / thing' (木头 mùtou wood, 石头 shítou stone, 骨头 gǔtou bone). Common: 头疼/头痛 (tóuténg/tóutòng, headache), 点头 (diǎntóu, nod), 摇头 (yáotóu, shake head). Measure word for cattle: 一头牛 (one head of cattle).

content.name: hair_on_head
content.side_e: Tā de tóufa hěn cháng.
content.side_f: Her hair is very long.
content.side_g: 头 (tóu, head) + 发 (fà, hair) — pronounced tóufa with the second syllable typically toneless in speech. Only refers to hair on the head; body hair is 汗毛 (hànmáo) and facial hair is 胡子 (húzi). Note 发 has two readings: 发 (fà) = hair, 发 (fā) = to send/emit (发短信 fā duǎnxìn, send a text). Useful: 剪头发 (jiǎn tóufa, cut hair), 染头发 (rǎn tóufa, dye hair).

content.name: tongue
content.side_e: Wǒ de shétou bèi tàng dào le.
content.side_f: I burned my tongue.
content.side_g: 舌 (shé, tongue) + 头 (tou, noun-softening suffix, toneless here). Same softening pattern as 木头 (mùtou, wood), 骨头 (gǔtou, bone). Idiom: 口干舌燥 (kǒu gān shé zào, lit. 'mouth dry tongue parched' = parched/thirsty). Tongue twister = 绕口令 (ràokǒulìng).

dialogue_001.title: Attending the Birthday Party
dialogue_001.description: Textbook Dialogue II: Wang Peng and Li You arrive at Gao Xiaoyin's birthday party

lines.a: 王朋，李友，快进来。
lines.b: Wáng Péng, Lǐ Yǒu, kuài jìn lai.
lines.c: Wang Peng, Li You, come in quickly.

lines.a: 小音，祝你生日快乐！这是送给你的生日礼物。
lines.b: Xiǎoyīn, zhù nǐ shēngrì kuàilè! Zhè shì sòng gěi nǐ de shēngrì lǐwù.
lines.c: Xiaoyin, happy birthday! This is a birthday gift for you.

lines.a: 谢谢！太好了！我一直想买这本书。带这么多东西，你们太客气了。
lines.b: Xièxiè! Tài hǎo le! Wǒ yìzhí xiǎng mǎi zhè běn shū. Dài zhème duō dōngxi, nǐmen tài kèqi le.
lines.c: Thank you! That's wonderful! I've been wanting to buy this book. Bringing so many things — you're too polite.

lines.a: 哥哥，李友，你们来了。
lines.b: Gēge, Lǐ Yǒu, nǐmen lái le.
lines.c: Big brother, Li You, you're here.

lines.a: 啊。小红，你怎么样？
lines.b: À. Xiǎo Hóng, nǐ zěnmeyàng?
lines.c: Oh. Xiao Hong, how are you?

lines.a: 我很好。每天都在学英文。
lines.b: Wǒ hěn hǎo. Měitiān dōu zài xué Yīngwén.
lines.c: I'm fine. Every day I'm studying English.

lines.a: 小红，你每天练习英文练习多长时间？
lines.b: Xiǎo Hóng, nǐ měitiān liànxí Yīngwén liànxí duō cháng shíjiān?
lines.c: Xiao Hong, how long do you practice English every day?

lines.a: 三个半钟头。还看两个钟头的英文电视。
lines.b: Sān ge bàn zhōngtóu. Hái kàn liǎng ge zhōngtóu de Yīngwén diànshì.
lines.c: Three and a half hours. I also watch two hours of English TV.

lines.a: 哎，你们两个是什么时候到的？
lines.b: Āi, nǐmen liǎng ge shì shénme shíhou dào de?
lines.c: Hey, when did you two arrive?

lines.a: 刚到。
lines.b: Gāng dào.
lines.c: Just arrived.

lines.a: 白英爱没跟你们一起来吗？
lines.b: Bái Yīng'ài méi gēn nǐmen yìqǐ lái ma?
lines.c: Didn't Bai Ying'ai come with you?

lines.a: 她还没来？我以为她已经来了。
lines.b: Tā hái méi lái? Wǒ yǐwéi tā yǐjīng lái le.
lines.c: She hasn't come yet? I thought she had already arrived.

lines.a: 王朋，李友，来，我给你们介绍一下，这是我表姐海伦，这是她的儿子汤姆。
lines.b: Wáng Péng, Lǐ Yǒu, lái, wǒ gěi nǐmen jièshào yí xià, zhè shì wǒ biǎojiě Hǎilún, zhè shì tā de érzi Tāngmǔ.
lines.c: Wang Peng, Li You, come, let me introduce you. This is my older cousin Helen, and this is her son Tom.

lines.a: 你好，海伦。
lines.b: Nǐ hǎo, Hǎilún.
lines.c: Hello, Helen.

lines.a: 你好，王朋。文中和小音都说你又聪明又用功。
lines.b: Nǐ hǎo, Wáng Péng. Wénzhōng hé Xiǎoyīn dōu shuō nǐ yòu cōngming yòu yònggōng.
lines.c: Hello, Wang Peng. Wenzhong and Xiaoyin both say you are both smart and hardworking.

lines.a: 哪里，哪里。你的中文说得真好，是在哪儿学的？
lines.b: Nǎli, nǎli. Nǐ de Zhōngwén shuō de zhēn hǎo, shì zài nǎr xué de?
lines.c: You flatter me. Your Chinese is really good — where did you learn it?

lines.a: 在暑期班学的。
lines.b: Zài shǔqī bān xué de.
lines.c: I learned it in summer school.

lines.a: 哎，汤姆长得真可爱！你们看，他笑了。他几岁了？
lines.b: Āi, Tāngmǔ zhǎng de zhēn kě'ài! Nǐmen kàn, tā xiào le. Tā jǐ suì le?
lines.c: Oh, Tom looks really cute! Look — he's smiling. How old is he?

lines.a: 刚一岁，是去年生的，属狗。
lines.b: Gāng yí suì, shì qùnián shēng de, shǔ gǒu.
lines.c: Just one year old. He was born last year — he's a Dog (zodiac).

lines.a: 你们看，他的脸圆圆的，眼睛大大的，鼻子高高的，嘴不大也不小，长得很像海伦。
lines.b: Nǐmen kàn, tā de liǎn yuán yuán de, yǎnjing dà dà de, bízi gāo gāo de, zuǐ bú dà yě bù xiǎo, zhǎng de hěn xiàng Hǎilún.
lines.c: Look, his face is round, his eyes are big, his nose is high, his mouth is neither big nor small — he really looks like Helen.

lines.a: 妈妈这么漂亮，儿子长大一定也很帅。
lines.b: Māma zhème piàoliang, érzi zhǎng dà yídìng yě hěn shuài.
lines.c: With such a pretty mom, when the son grows up he will definitely be handsome too.

lines.a: 来，来，来，我们吃蛋糕吧。
lines.b: Lái, lái, lái, wǒmen chī dàngāo ba.
lines.c: Come on, come on, let's eat cake.

lines.a: 等等白英爱吧。她最爱吃蛋糕。
lines.b: Děng děng Bái Yīng'ài ba. Tā zuì ài chī dàngāo.
lines.c: Let's wait for Bai Ying'ai. She loves cake the most.

dialogue_002.title: Talking About Zodiac Signs
dialogue_002.description: A small-talk exchange about Chinese zodiac years and ages

lines.a: 小红，你属什么？
lines.b: Xiǎo Hóng, nǐ shǔ shénme?
lines.c: Xiao Hong, what's your zodiac sign?

lines.a: 我属狗，是去年生的吗？不，我已经十几岁了。
lines.b: Wǒ shǔ gǒu, shì qùnián shēng de ma? Bù, wǒ yǐjīng shí jǐ suì le.
lines.c: I'm a Dog. Was I born last year? No, I'm already in my teens.

wordAlignments.english: am born in the year of

lines.a: 你又聪明又用功，长大一定很帅。
lines.b: Nǐ yòu cōngming yòu yònggōng, zhǎng dà yídìng hěn shuài.
lines.c: You're both smart and hardworking — you'll definitely be handsome when you grow up.

lines.a: 哪里，哪里。汤姆的脸圆圆的，眼睛大大的，真可爱。
lines.b: Nǎli, nǎli. Tāngmǔ de liǎn yuán yuán de, yǎnjing dà dà de, zhēn kě'ài.
lines.c: You flatter me. Tom's face is round and his eyes are big — really cute.

lines.a: 他鼻子高高的，嘴小小的，长得很像海伦。
lines.b: Tā bízi gāo gāo de, zuǐ xiǎo xiǎo de, zhǎng de hěn xiàng Hǎilún.
lines.c: His nose is high and his mouth is small — he really looks like Helen.

grammar_001.title: Time-Duration: Subject + Verb + (了) + Duration
grammar_001.description: Stating how long an action lasts; with an object, the pattern is V + 了 + Duration + (的) + Object

lines.a: 我每天学三个钟头中文。
lines.b: Wǒ měitiān xué sān ge zhōngtóu Zhōngwén.
lines.c: I study Chinese three hours every day.

lines.a: 他看了两个钟头的电视。
lines.b: Tā kàn le liǎng ge zhōngtóu de diànshì.
lines.c: He watched two hours of TV.

lines.a: 我上了四个星期的暑期班。
lines.b: Wǒ shàng le sì ge xīngqī de shǔqī bān.
lines.c: I took four weeks of summer school.

lines.a: 她每天练习英文练习三个半钟头。
lines.b: Tā měitiān liànxí Yīngwén liànxí sān ge bàn zhōngtóu.
lines.c: She practices English for three and a half hours every day.

lines.a: 老高想在上海住一年。
lines.b: Lǎo Gāo xiǎng zài Shànghǎi zhù yì nián.
lines.c: Old Gao wants to live in Shanghai for one year.

grammar_002.title: 是…的 Sentences for Past Actions
grammar_002.description: Use 是…的 to highlight time, place, or manner of a known past event

lines.a: 你是什么时候到的？
lines.b: Nǐ shì shénme shíhou dào de?
lines.c: When did you arrive?

lines.a: 我是昨天到的。
lines.b: Wǒ shì zuótiān dào de.
lines.c: I arrived yesterday.

lines.a: 你的中文是在哪儿学的？
lines.b: Nǐ de Zhōngwén shì zài nǎr xué de?
lines.c: Where did you learn your Chinese?

lines.a: 我是在暑期班学的。
lines.b: Wǒ shì zài shǔqī bān xué de.
lines.c: I learned it in summer school.

lines.a: 汤姆是去年生的。
lines.b: Tāngmǔ shì qùnián shēng de.
lines.c: Tom was born last year.

lines.a: 你是跟谁一起来的？
lines.b: Nǐ shì gēn shéi yìqǐ lái de?
lines.c: Who did you come with?

grammar_003.title: 还 (hái) — Still / Yet
grammar_003.description: 还 indicates an action or state continues, or has not yet happened (with negation)

lines.a: 上午十一点了，他还在睡觉。
lines.b: Shàngwǔ shíyī diǎn le, tā hái zài shuì jiào.
lines.c: It's 11 a.m. and he's still sleeping.

lines.a: 她还没来。
lines.b: Tā hái méi lái.
lines.c: She hasn't come yet.

lines.a: 我以为她已经来了，可是她还没到。
lines.b: Wǒ yǐwéi tā yǐjīng lái le, kěshì tā hái méi dào.
lines.c: I thought she had already come, but she still hasn't arrived.

lines.a: 他还想再看两个钟头的电视。
lines.b: Tā hái xiǎng zài kàn liǎng ge zhōngtóu de diànshì.
lines.c: He still wants to watch two more hours of TV.

grammar_004.title: 又…又… (yòu…yòu…) — Both…and…
grammar_004.description: Use 又 + Adj1 + 又 + Adj2 to describe two coexisting qualities of a subject

lines.a: 他又聪明又用功。
lines.b: Tā yòu cōngming yòu yònggōng.
lines.c: He is both smart and hardworking.

lines.a: 汤姆又可爱又像妈妈。
lines.b: Tāngmǔ yòu kě'ài yòu xiàng māma.
lines.c: Tom is both cute and looks like his mom.

lines.a: 这个蛋糕又大又好吃。
lines.b: Zhè ge dàngāo yòu dà yòu hǎo chī.
lines.c: This cake is both big and delicious.

lines.a: 他的脸又圆又可爱。
lines.b: Tā de liǎn yòu yuán yòu kě'ài.
lines.c: His face is both round and cute.

lines.a: 她又最爱吃蛋糕又最会做蛋糕。
lines.b: Tā yòu zuì ài chī dàngāo yòu zuì huì zuò dàngāo.
lines.c: She both loves eating cake the most and is best at making cake.
